speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Felix Pierson. This is roll  number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Felix, you said you didn't speak about your brother. So tell us about your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't remember much about my brother. And the hardest thing is I can't remember his face. There's no way to get a picture. But I know the general outline of this face, the color of his hair. And I know he was-- I was the quiet boy and he was the lively one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I cannot-- I'm trying so hard to try to remember his face, and I just can't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell them-- tell us his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his name-- we used to call him Lolick. But it was Loeser, probably the Hebrew name of Haleuser. And one thing that all the time that keeps coming back to me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he must have looked up to me, because wherever I went, he was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if there can be a humorous side, the  funny thing was one day he saw me talking to us schoolmate, to a girl. And he came running home to his-- to my parents saying, you must hear this, you know? He must-- this is Faftya. Faftya went on a date. I saw him with  a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he would always find something to bring to the parents about me, but not-- not in a complaining sense, but being proud of-- of his brother. And shortly before they deported us from our hometown, our father made for us suits from fabric that is usually made for grown up people. But this is what he had, and this is what he made. And we wore it practically most of the time because we were expecting to being deported from our town, because we saw trucks-- trucks with people from the vicinity passing our town on the way to Chelmno. We didn't know where they were going.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother was running this new suit. And as a young boy, he jumped over a fence and he tore his-- the back on his jacket. I went to one of the men that worked  for my father who was a very good tailor. And I asked him to try to sew up the hole that my father wouldn't recognize it-- notice it because he would be very upset with him and with me for letting this happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the deportation, after I came to the Lodz Ghetto, in the ghetto, they were-- there were fac-- clothing factories. There was one for grownups-- many for grownups-- and there was one for people under 16-- for children under 16 where we worked in a factory and produced uniforms for the Germans. I, knowing a little bit about tailoring, what I saw in my father's shop, became a foreman in this factory. And one morning, I saw a child, a young boy, about the age that my brother would have been, come in in a jacket-- in the jacket that my brother had torn jumping over the fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he   a Gentile child?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, there were only-- it was in the ghetto. It was Jewish-- Jewish children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you think he got it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This clo-- these clothing came-- was taken to the Lodz Ghetto and it was given out to people as a ration of clothing, because from-- they didn't bring in any new clothing. This is what they gave out to the population. And it so happened that this boy had this jacket. I recognized the tear on the back. I recognized the buttonhole that I sewed up for him. Of course, I was very upset over it, re-- realizing that my brother doesn't have the clothes. That means that it was taken from him. No one wanted yet to believe that I'll never see him again, because he went-- you know, I with my father was deported to the Lodz Ghetto. My brother and my mother were sent someplace else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us-- I'm not clear on that. How could it have happened you were separated so early? Just go back a bit. Your mother went some-- one place and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when they deported our town, they-- we expected it any day, but whenever it happens, it's still unexpected. Whoever could run and walk, walk and run, was chased into the Catholic church. I had a two-year-old cousin. But we all live in our grandfather's house in the ghetto. And our cou-- uncle also lived there and he had a two-year-old boy, which was his-- his name was the same as mine, Feiwel. And he was there for an appendix operation. And he was laying in the yard on a lounge. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The parents never had a chance to take this child with them. And during that time, we were told by people that were in town later on, helped to clean out the town from clothing and other stuff that would be sent out to the Lodz Ghetto and for other things more valuable were going-- going to the German-- that they have seen the child shot. He had-- you know, they shot him in his head in the alley, a two-year-old child. And the fact is that many years after being already in the United States, I have nightmares. I woke up in  the middle of the night. I used to scream [? Herneke, ?] [? Herneke, ?] don't forget the children, because that was on my mind what I had seen. During the deportation, that is when they killed my grandmother. She couldn't walk fast enough. A relative, my grandmother's brother, was shot, and the son of-- of my grandmother's brother, when the-- when they walked over to the German, he says, don't you keep pushing my grand-- my father. He says, if you say another word, I'm going to shoot him. He says, you're not going to shoot my father. You are going to shoot me. And he stood in front of his father. And he was shot. He shot him, and he shot his grand-- his grandfather, his father. And at that time, my grandmother was shot. And 6-- like I said before, 65 people were killed on the way to the church. We stayed for three days in the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at this point, you were together with your mother and brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire town was in the church except those that were killed. The entire town was in that Catholic church. We had to go in through a very narrow entrance, not through the main entrance where they open the gates, but a side entrance. They were standing there-- excuse me. They were standing there with those police sticks and the-- and the butts of the guns of the rifles, and were hitting everyone who walked in because you could only go through one or two at a time. Inside, without having any facilities-- hygienic facilities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We spent three days without any food. Thank god we had no food, so at least it kept people a little bit cleaner. I remember in that church, the rabbi performed a wedding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We prayed in the syn-- the church.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked my father how can we pray in a Catholic church? The crosses are all over. And my father said that the rabbi just told everyone that wherever there is a minion of Jews is a holy place and you can pray. You disregard all the crosses.

speaker: interviewee
text: After three days, Biebow, Himmler's son-in-law, came to our town. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he separated-- you know, we went all in one group, the way Mengele did in Auschwitz. He would-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, everyone would come in front of him. And he would say, you go back to the church-- to the church. And those that went back to the church, these were being sent away to Himmler for  extermination. The others were sent into a field. It was a ravine, because we were-- stay-- stayed there overnight. I don't remember exactly the number. It's someplace between 600 or 800 people, closer to 600. I really don't know and I wouldn't want to mistake the number. But we stayed overnight and then the next day we were taken to  the railroad and traveled to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that journey?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. I really-- we came at the central place where we sorted out, you know, we were sent to different places to stay. We didn't-- didn't take anything except what was on our back. Nothing-- no package-- not the smallest package in the hand. Only what we had on our back-- back. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew that those-- because those that they selected to Lodz-- to Ghetto Lodz-- were only strong people, young people. And here I have to-- and I remember that I was wearing pullover blouse that my father made for me in a navy fabric. But I had stuck a white needle-- I mean a needle with white thread. And I was at that time-- in '44 I was 16 years old-- and I said, schneider, which means tailor, because I-- I was afraid I was too young to be sent with the men. So he saw the white thread, so he sent me-- you know, he said to go to the right. And before me-- my father was before me. And  in three days, my father had grown a beard. And he became gray when he was fighting in 1920. He was a young man. He was fighting for the independence of Poland against the Russians. And at that time, he turned gray. So he had sort of a gray beard and he looked like an old man. So I don't know what came over him. I just remembered that he had in the pocket a document with this picture that he made the picture in  a spa. And he looked very young, very healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I put it in front of his face saying schneidermeister. And this is how we both went with those that were-- were supposed to-- to go to work. And we never heard about our-- you know, what happened to the other people-- to our, in this case, my mother, and my brother, and all  the relatives, and the rest of the town. How long were you in the Lodz Ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- till '44. Till August '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long-- you were there longer than you were in the Lesk-- Lask Ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe not-- about the same-- about the same time. There was also about--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the-- were-- you haven't told me about the conditions in ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in the Lask Ghetto, you know, before we were deported, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at first, it wasn't, you know, everyone still had some connections and everyone still have some food hidden away. And we were working for the peasants around in the vicinity. So if we make a pair of pants for them, they would bring some food. But things started to get tighter and worse. And I remember the first time that my mother had cooked horse meat. And you know, it wasn't kosher. But it was no problem anymore. I remember, it tasted like-- like baked  goods. It was delicious. Because you get used-- you get used to things. And we were sitting on the attic, I and my father, and made these clothes by hand. They took-- Germans took away all the machinery from us, because in town, they had created a clothing factory where we, again, were making clothing for German civilian companies. And then we made uniforms. So it's what-- that is what it's going to be. If we have to work during the-- work isn't the worst thing. We had a roof on our head. We had before four rooms that we lived in the apartment, plus the factory upstairs. And here we lived all in one room, which was fine. No one complained about it. We came home from work. We still had our-- you know, our--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your mother and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and  my brother up-- up to-- up to the deportation. And the ghetto was getting worse and worse. And like I said, they-- they-- what they did is knocked down our spirits, our-- our self-respect. And they tried to scare us in every way they could. They would take-- send out groups of people to go to work outside the ghetto to the Germans. And one day, two young brothers came back from work. And they carried some potatoes in their pockets and in their shirt, like you must have seen the shows about the different-- you know, I've seen it a number of times. They showed it in the Warsaw Ghetto where the Germans caught them. But at that time, there were no border-- there were no wires outside the ghetto. You could have smuggled in through a number of ways. And there were Jewish policeman standing and-- and the Germans. But this was-- this was a set up. They let these boys take the potatoes, and they were waiting for-- follow-- they must have followed them. Because when they came in the ghetto, they stopped them. They opened their shirts and the potatoes fell out. And they arrested these two boys, and a few days later, they took the entire town out to the fields and-- outside the town and hung these two young boys in front of everybody, everybody would see. And they started to take by groups people and send away in work camps. And from time to time, we heard that people died. No one-- we didn't know if they were shot or whatever happened. We have seen the conditions of the-- you know, of the living conditions constantly deteriorate. And we heard terrified, horrible stories of what is happening about those people that are being sent away from other towns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Did you have any concept of-- of the whole plan of extermination?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No way, in no way, because people were-- people from our town ran away to the Russian side of the-- of the side of the Russians that occupied Poland. And they came back saying how bad it is there. So when we so these people coming back swollen, filthy. So why should we run? And besides, we lived in a vicinity where you didn't expect any help from the Poles-- from our Polish neighbors. There was one that had offered my father to hide-- that he could hide out in his place, but we didn't have confidence that he really meant it. But what happened, as conditions got worse and worse, the population got smaller and smaller in town. And then they started the arrests and the beatings. Like one day, one of those people-- there were some Jewish people that worked, you know, with the Germans. And I have in particular in mind one person that used to sell sodas, before the war, with a-- on horse and buggy. And he had  a Polish assistant. And these two people, they worked with the Germans. They pointed out who are the rich people in town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how rich could a tailor be? We were comfortable. We were never hungry. So they knew that my-- my father was a custom tailor. In other words, we sold suits from our own fabric. So we had to have fabric hidden someplace. And we had to have money hidden someplace. So they pulled my father-- These are the Jews who pointed you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: One-- one Jew. One.

speaker: interviewer
text: One Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: One. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was taken-- and my father was taken to the gendarmerie, which was, at that time, the military police. When my father came back, his back was cut like spaghetti strips. And it wasn't-- I know the doctor came-- you know, the Jewish-- a Jewish doctor. He wasn't a doctor, a felcher, which is, in my mind, he practically knew everything what a doctor did. But at that time, probably not a fully graduated, you know, doctor. And he put compresses and whatever he could, he put on my father's back. But the rest of us at home, we felt that our shield of protection-- our father-- was offended, was hurt. They took this away from us, and I can't begin to describe that feeling of not so much for our father, that we felt our family is collapsing. My father that we always considered the stronger and the protector, and all of a sudden, he was taken in by these people and beaten up to that way-- that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the conditions in town kept constantly deteriorating. More and more people were sent away to work. More people that were fine, comfortable, intelligent people were being destroyed, in spiritually and physically. And-- and one day, on the Purim day-- it was Purim-- they knew exactly how to hurt us, because everything that happened was in a Jew-- most of the worst things that used to happen were on a Jewish holiday. That was Purim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these-- went to-- the Gendarmes went to the-- to our rabbi and said, today, today is the holiday that the Jews celebrate for hanging Haman. Haman. He said, we want to show you that we are more humane than the Jews are. On this holiday, we  are not going to hang 10 people. We   will only hang two. And we want you to give us the two people. And-- and the-- and they said, we will come back tomorrow, and I want you to name us two people that we should hang them. He said-- rabbi said, you don't have to come back tomorrow. I'll give you right now. Take me and my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they hit him very-- you know, they beat them up very badly. How dare   he talk to them like that? And they took him out and they said, OK, we're going to hang you. And they pulled him up and dropped him, pulled him up and dropped him a number of times.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there at the square when they did that but we heard  a number of-- that they did it a number of times. And at the end, when he was already-- ready to pass out, some people took him home. They let them live, which, in a way, you know, sometimes to live but being tortured, your dignity is being, you know, degraded. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the rabbi would have been happy if they would have hang him that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So things like that were going on constantly, constantly, till, you know, you-- you lose every self-respect. I have heard people saying, why didn't you fight back? You know? First of all, it wasn't with what to fight back, and we were surrounded by enemies, by the-- by the Volksdeutsche, the Polish people, that all their-- all our lives we knew they were Poles. All of a sudden, they turned out to be Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I had a night when we lived in our house, there was a lady that worked for the government. And every morning, if I would see her go to work, I would say good morning-- [POLISH] Miss Grenuvna. That's the Polish expression for a miss if she's not married. She was uvna. That's how it ended. And during the occupation, I said the same thing to her. I says, you're calling me the wrong name. My name is Fraulein Green. So they're all, you know-- they all all of a sudden became-- became Germans. They chased-- they took over the Jewish apartments. They came constantly in the ghetto to rob whatever they-- they-- they could. And it was getting from bad to worse. There weren't really many people left to be sent away to the-- work-- people in working-- working-- working condition. Till they came to the-- you know-- there was, in two years, there isn't so much time spent to notice how this thing, you know, these things go from bad to-- but they did. People lost-- lost members of their family. And in '42, they came in Octo-- August on the 24th. And they deported everyone from our home-- from our hometown. And those that didn't go to the Lodz Ghetto were sent to Chelmno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among them your mother and brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my brother and most of those people in our town. And when I went in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was August. We went for yahrzeit. The Jews left at that time and those that came to-- from other-- from other places to Lask, we all fit-- in a truck. Not in a truck. It was only halfway filled. And we came to-- to Chelmno to  say Kaddish [? over there. ?] Well, you can imagine in 1945 when the wounds were so fresh and everyone was missing everyone, what was going on there by that Kaddish. But I walked around and I saw these burned pieces of bones laying shattered around, all over the place. I put them in a-- in a piece of paper. I collected them and I took them with me. And later on, when I was already back in Germany, I gave these bones to this rabbi-- our rabbi. The rabbi that I mentioned before that he was hung and let down. Their son lived in Israel. So he came to visit to Germany. And I gave him this-- these bones and they were put [INAUDIBLE], you know. They were put under a  monument in Israel where we have [INAUDIBLE], a beautiful monument in the memory of our, you know victims of the Holocaust. And we put these bones underneath this monument. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, looking around at that place where it happened, you know, there were these Polish people that were there during the time that we came for the yahrzeit, they had told us in what a primitive way, at the beginning. The people were put on piles and put gasoline on them and burned until they built this-- these crematoria. And when I went again to visit that place in 19-- either '78 or '79, there was a big sign in front, [INAUDIBLE] describing who organized this place, Chelmno. Who-- how many people came from each town. I can't understand how a town was named there. But the Polish people did that, and they probably were given the wrong information, because they put up a city block-- a long monument that I happen to-- I have a picture that a professional photographer took for me-- for me. And on one side of that big, tremendous monument, it says that the city of Kolo and Dabie-- because that's where Chelmno is situated between those two cities-- they have contributed money for this monument.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the front side, you see carved out you know, Jews spent, old and young, and the Polish sign, [POLISH] which means, we remember. But on the back of that monument, it says, and I'll try to remember as close as I can. It said, they brought us here from the old to the infant.

speaker: interviewee
text: They tortured us. They burned us. They shot us. They killed us in ev-- in every way. We beg our future brothers never to forget what happened here, and those that witness it-- witnessed it, to tell it to the world. It's a national shrine now in Poland. It's in the middle of nowhere, on a road where, if you don't know exactly what you're looking for, you can hardly find it. But right now, there is a lady there that she's a real righteous person, whatever religion she is. She's a-- she wears a black dress constantly like she would be in mourning. And she is so dedicated to this place, because I have a video that we send representatives from our society, from our [INAUDIBLE], from our-- people from our town from Israel, where they put up a plaque in memory of the people-- people from our town that were exterminated in such a cruel way in this place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that is the story of Chelmno. When  I came there the last time in '79 with the photographer, but-- because I knew I couldn't take pictures-- and a taxi driver, they had to carry me off. It's one thing to know about the place. And it's another thing when your feet stand on that ground. That ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took the-- the ashes and threw them in River Warta. It's called 'Varta' In Polish with a 'V.' And this-- this-- these ashes are laying in this river with a hard-- with a fast current that's probably now in the Baltic Ocean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, we'll go on  on the next tape.
